title: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 8.1 — Operational planning: change advisory board (CAB) approval evidence
exclusions: Does NOT approve or reject changes; attests that 'normal' and 'emergency' changes have the required CAB approval record before implementation.
evidence: Saved search 'iso_27001_8_1_cab_approval' (hourly) emits per change: change_id, type, implemented_at, cab_approved_at, approval_met, approver, to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Clause 8.1 Operational planning and control — CAB approval adherence is the direct control signal.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO/IEC 27001 8.1 (Operational planning) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.55: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 8.1 — Operational planning: change advisory board (CAB) approval evidence.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.6.55' running on index=change sourcetype=servicenow:change; approver-role lookup cab_roles.csv., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that SOC-2 CC8.1 (Change management) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.55: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 8.1 — Operational planning: change advisory board (CAB) approval evidence.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.6.55' running on index=change sourcetype=servicenow:change; approver-role lookup cab_roles.csv., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Closing a change with approval_status='rejected' but state='implemented' returns approval_met=0.
controlTest.negativeScenario: A change approved by a CAB-role user before implementation is absent.

dataSources: `index=change` sourcetype=servicenow:change; approver-role lookup `cab_roles.csv`. (Splunkbase 1621 — Splunk CIM Add-on)
spl:
index=change sourcetype=servicenow:change state IN (implemented,closed) earliest=-7d
| stats latest(type) AS type latest(_time) AS implemented_at latest(approval_status) AS approval_status latest(approver) AS approver latest(approval_date) AS cab_approved_at BY change_id
| lookup cab_roles.csv approver OUTPUT is_authorised_cab
| eval approval_met=if(type="emergency",1,if(approval_status="approved" AND is_authorised_cab=1 AND cab_approved_at<=implemented_at,1,0))
| where approval_met=0
| table change_id type implemented_at cab_approved_at approver approval_met
description: Flags implemented changes that were not approved by an authorised CAB member prior to implementation. Clause 8.1 requires the organisation to plan, implement and control processes to meet information-security requirements — CAB approval is the direct control.
value: Turns the 'post-implementation review' into a pre-emptive signal. The Head of IT Operations sees the breach list within the hour.
implementation: (1) Export ServiceNow change events; (2) maintain cab_roles.csv listing authorised approvers; (3) schedule UC hourly; (4) approval_met=0 opens a deficiency record with 7d remediation; (5) monthly Audit Committee report. Indexes required: `index=change`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=servicenow:change`, `sourcetype=servicenow:change;`.
visualization: Table of unapproved changes, bar chart of breach count by team, single value 'unapproved changes in last 7d'. Key fields: `approval_met`, `approval_status`, `approver`, `cab_approved_at`, `change_id`, `implemented_at`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Assessment cycle alignment** — Control assessments following annual cycles may show brief non-current periods between assessment completion and report publication.

2. **Finding remediation within SLA** — Open findings within their documented remediation timeline are being addressed per policy, not neglected or overlooked.

3. **Framework version transitions** — Migration between control framework versions (ISO 27001:2013 to 2022) creates temporary mapping gaps during the documented transition period.

4. **Accepted residual risk items** — Findings formally accepted through the risk management governance process with documented compensating controls and management sign-off.

5. **Evidence collection cadence differences** — Controls with different evidence generation frequencies (real-time vs. quarterly) show varying currency in compliance dashboards.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`iso27001_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by control owner, risk acceptance reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries at each ISMS management review cycle.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `change` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `cab_roles.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('cab_roles.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `8.1`. The control objective is: "Evidence that ISO/IEC 27001 8.1 (Operational planning) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.55: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 8.1 — Operational planning: change advisory board (CAB) approval evidence." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `change` under `servicenow:change`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Export ServiceNow change events. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

• **Hint 2:** maintain cab_roles.csv listing authorised approvers. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 3:** schedule UC hourly. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** approval_met=0 opens a deficiency record with 7d remediation. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** monthly Audit Committee report. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 8.1 — Operational planning: change advisory board (CAB) approval evidence," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of ISO/IEC 27001 control activity.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `cab_roles.csv` — the enrichment table that adds compliance-relevant business context to raw events. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`is_authorised_cab`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `approver`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `cab_roles.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval approver = lower(approver)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 7 days.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.6.55: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 8.1 — Operational planning: change]
cron_schedule = 0 2 * * 1
dispatch.earliest_time = -7d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.6.55,reg=ISO/IEC_27001
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=change sourcetype=servicenow:change earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `change_id`, `approver`, `approval_status`, `state`, `_time`. Run:

```spl
index=change sourcetype=servicenow:change earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table change_id, approver, approval_status, state, _time
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `cab_roles.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup cab_roles.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.6.55: ISO/IEC 27001 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Unapproved Changes (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Bar Chart Of Breach Count By Team (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single Value 'Unapproved Changes In Last 7D' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** Check the lookup's case sensitivity (Splunk lookups are case-sensitive by default) and null handling — a null on the input side produces a silent miss, not an error.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**ISO/IEC 27001-specific failure modes:**

• Statement of Applicability (SoA) drift: ISO 27001 requires a SoA listing which Annex A controls are applicable and which are excluded with justification. A detection for a control excluded in the SoA adds noise to the audit evidence pile; reconcile UC-to-SoA mapping annually. Maintain `iso27001_soa.csv` with current applicability.

• Context of the organisation (Cl. 4): controls calibrated for a 50-person company's threat model do not suit a 5000-person multinational's. Detection thresholds, log retention, and escalation paths must align with the organisation's ISMS scope — a single 'enterprise default' for ISO 27001 is a tell-tale sign of a cookie-cutter implementation.

• Internal audit programme (Cl. 9.2) evidence: detections that support internal audits must produce records that can be attached to audit evidence packs. Tag the alert payload with audit_cycle=YYYY-HN so evidence bundling is trivial.

• Management review inputs (Cl. 9.3): control effectiveness metrics must be reviewable at management review. A detection that only raises alerts (no trend metric) cannot be brought to the management meeting; schedule a companion report that produces trend data per control.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 8.1 — Operational planning: change advisory board (CAB) approval evidence. ISO 27001 tells us what good security management looks like, and we document that we follow it.
